# **Dynasty Genius UI/UX Architecture Spec: Phase 16 to 17 Transition**

## **Architectural Foundations and the Interface Epistemology**

The evolution of the Dynasty Genius platform from the Phase 16 architecture into the comprehensive Phase 17 expansion requires a fundamental restructuring of the user interface's underlying epistemology. Phase 16 focused heavily on resolving 2026 biological age blockers, evaluating discrete translatable college production signals, and finalizing the mathematical validations for the draft-capital transform via a competitive model bake-off.1 Transitioning into Phase 17—which introduces the full Sleeper universe valuation matrix, dynamic team value matrices, the league opportunity map, and Market Divergence v2—demands an interface that acts not as a passive display of statistical outputs, but as a rigid, governance-bound decision support system.1  
The absolute primary directive governing this architectural leap is the strict and uncompromising separation of evidence from inference.5 The frontend presentation layer exists strictly to serve the model outputs and the established mathematical governance rules.5 The system is permanently prohibited from generating UI-first recommendations. Every pixel rendered on the decision surface must directly reflect a validated programmatic constraint or an active engine output.4 To maintain the integrity of the predictive engines, the UI must never front-run the data science; there can be no production model changes implicitly forced by UI design requirements without rigorous, isolated backend validation.4  
This transition relies completely on the full adoption of the "Glass Box" philosophy, heavily drawing from Explainable AI (XAI) frameworks deployed in clinical decision support and highly regulated financial technology environments.5 Traditional dynasty evaluation platforms operate as opaque black boxes, delivering aggregated player value scores stripped of all contextual mechanism. This structural opacity invites immediate user skepticism, strips away actionable strategic intelligence, and inherently masks the massive mathematical volatility associated with forecasting athletic performance over a multi-year horizon.5  
To establish a sophisticated, trustworthy decision surface, the system must decompose the aggregated Dynasty Value Score (DVS) into its constituent programmatic elements: Baseline Model Forecast, Market Price Overlay, Contextual Roster Fit, and Mathematical Uncertainty.5 The user interface serves as the terminal point for algorithmic accountability.5 If a wide receiver's baseline value is structurally elevated by the model, the UI must explicitly map the programmatic influence of specific, quantifiable metrics directly onto the visual surface.5 The interface must utilize analytical techniques akin to Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to reveal the isolated weight of individual features.5 By making the programmatic influence of age, draft capital, and production directly legible on the visual interface, the system ensures the user understands precisely why an asset is valued at a specific threshold.5

### **The Anti-Speed Protocol and Cognitive Bias Mitigation**

Confirmation bias and survivorship bias remain the most pervasive and destructive structural flaws in human-driven dynasty asset management.2 Managers systematically seek out data points that validate their preconceived narratives regarding a player, while actively ignoring or actively discounting contradictory statistical evidence.5 Furthermore, users exhibit a dangerous "speed bias," rushing to execute transactions based on superficial metrics without interrogating the underlying risk profile.5  
To combat this, the Dynasty Genius UI must enforce an "Anti-Speed Protocol" via the programmatic generation of a "Steel-Manned" Counter-Argument.2 Every valuation recommendation, arbitrage signal, or roster evaluation action generated by the backend engines must be instantaneously paired with its strongest data-driven refutation.2 Underneath the primary valuation card, the interface is mandated to dynamically output a Red Team case.2  
For instance, if the system generates a high-conviction signal to acquire a rookie running back based on elite first-round draft capital and a 9.1 Yards Per Carry (YPC) against light boxes, the UI must parallel process the opposing metrics and force them into the visual hierarchy.5 The layout must prominently display the counter-narrative, such as the presence of a severe committee backfield context, poor pass-blocking metrics, or a bottom-5 run-blocking offensive line grade.5 By forcing the manager to immediately confront the full, unvarnished probabilistic risk profile of the asset, the system actively prevents false certainty and ensures decisions are highly defensible across a three-to-seven-year operational horizon.5

### **Strict Linguistic Governance: The Prohibition of Definitive Tiers**

A foundational cornerstone of the Phase 16 to 17 visual standard is the strict, system-wide ban on definitive action verbs and reductive subjective labeling.2 The system is explicitly prohibited from displaying generic, imperative language such as "Sell Now," "Must Buy," "Hold," or "Acquire".3 Furthermore, reductive tier classifications like "Bust," "Elite," "Generational," or "Trash" are permanently banned from the UI component library and all associated text strings.2  
Imposing artificial, deterministic certainty on a fundamentally stochastic environment permanently damages the long-term credibility of the underlying models.5 Natural variance inevitably occurs; an "elite" prospect will tear an ACL, and a "bust" prospect will inherit a starting role due to depth chart attrition. When a system utilizes deterministic language and fails, user trust erodes instantaneously.6 The platform's sole mandate is to present calculated probabilities, historical distributions, and mathematical variance boundaries.5 The human manager must remain the final arbiter of the transaction, interpreting the evidence without the system dictating the final verdict.2

## **High-Density Typography and Financial UX Heuristics**

The visual benchmark for the Dynasty Genius platform must completely diverge from contemporary consumer sports applications, gamified fantasy interfaces, and minimalist web design.8 The target user is an analytical operator engaging in complex asset management under finite resource constraints; therefore, the interface must prioritize high data density, professional camouflage, and rigorous, earned visual hierarchy.8 The architectural inspiration for this density is derived directly from institutional financial technology, specifically the layout logic and interaction models of the Bloomberg Terminal.8

### **Structural Legibility and Professional Camouflage**

In high-stakes analytical environments, aggressive simplification is a critical UX anti-pattern.9 Hiding underlying numerical data behind overly friendly visual components or prioritizing aesthetic approachability over raw legibility destroys the fundamental utility of the dashboard.9 Users engaging with a team value matrix or a multi-year forecast do not want the data simplified; they want it structured logically.9 Every single pixel on the Dynasty Genius decision surface must be strictly accountable.9 Visual hierarchy must be earned strictly through data importance, statistical deviation, or temporal urgency, never through arbitrary aesthetic decoration.9  
The interface must leverage a "stealth UI" approach, often characterized as "professional camouflage".10 This design language heavily utilizes monospaced typography, strict grid-based spatial mapping, and low-saturation dark mode color palettes.10 Monospaced fonts are absolutely critical; they ensure that tabular numerical data aligns perfectly across vertical axes, allowing the human eye to rapidly scan, compare, and process hundreds of values down a column without experiencing tracking errors or visual fatigue.10 By stripping away gamified icons and forcing data into rigid grid constraints, the interface inherits a clinical, system-monitor aesthetic that reinforces the analytical gravity of the data being presented.10  
Furthermore, the deployment of a low-saturation foundation allows critical exception flags—such as severe market divergence signals, arbitrage opportunities, or biological age cliff warnings—to command immediate pre-attentive focus through the highly restricted use of high-contrast accent colors.9 Color must never be utilized as mere branding in this application.9 In a financialized UI, color is a highly potent, functional signal.9 A red indicator within a transaction model carries the exact psychological weight of a severe risk alert or a negative portfolio swing, and must be deployed with extreme restraint.9 Green and red must strictly indicate positive and negative mathematical vectors, not brand identity.9

### **Friction as a Strategic Trust Mechanism**

Within standard consumer applications, user friction is universally viewed as a fundamental flaw—an obstacle to be mercilessly eliminated by UX designers.9 However, in systems governing high-leverage asset transactions, carefully calibrated friction serves as an essential trust mechanism.9 When an interface processes a transaction, evaluates a complex trade, or initiates a structural shift in portfolio allocation, an entirely frictionless, one-click experience conveys a dangerous lack of systemic gravity.9 It subconsciously signals to the user that the system is not taking the action seriously.9  
In the Dynasty Genius Trade Lab, the introduction of intentional visual friction during trade evaluation is mandatory.9 Utilizing confirmation screens that pause the user, strictly mapping the exact multi-year portfolio impact, rendering the specific roster cut penalties, and requiring a deliberate secondary interaction before proceeding, signals that the system is appropriately careful with consequential actions.9 UX friction must be heavily applied to irreversible actions, major asset liquidations, and significant roster consolidations.9 Conversely, friction must remain completely absent from routine tasks such as data exploration, sorting, and query filtering.9 Knowing precisely where to slow the user down, and where to instantly get out of their way, defines expert-level system design.9

## **DOM Virtualization and Hyper-Scale Performance**

Handling the massive data matrices required for deep roster context, full league opportunity mapping, and Phase 17's total Sleeper universe valuation requires specialized frontend engineering to prevent catastrophic browser failure.11 The standard browser rendering pipeline is fundamentally not designed to concurrently manage tens of thousands of complex DOM node structures.11 If the application attempts to render every player, prospect, and historical data point simultaneously, the browser will spend exponentially more CPU cycles calculating styles and layout geometries for off-screen elements than on the data the user actually sees, resulting in severe interface latency and system crashes.11  
As the platform transitions to tracking thousands of discrete player entities across varying time horizons, the UI must implement strict fixed-height virtualization.11 By analyzing data architectures from hyper-scale environments like Spotify—which seamlessly ingests over one trillion telemetry events daily via highly scalable, at-least-once message buffers like Google Cloud Pub/Sub—it becomes clear that frontend interfaces must match backend efficiency.12 The frontend cannot block the analytical pipeline.  
Through UI virtualization, the application creates a standard external scroll container that calculates the theoretical maximum height of the entire dataset, establishing a phantom scrollbar to maintain traditional user navigation mechanics.11 However, the DOM strictly renders only the limited subset of data items currently visible within the active user viewport, plus a small buffer of sentinel nodes.11  
The virtualizer algorithm natively tracks positional geometry using ![][image1] random access mathematics, where the specific offset of any row is simply calculated as the index multiplied by the fixed row height.11 As the user scrolls through the full Sleeper universe, the system continuously recycles standard component nodes.11 By triggering the compositor and paint phases selectively, rather than initiating full, expensive browser layout recalculations, the application ensures instantaneous analytical fluidity regardless of the underlying dataset's volume.11 This infinite scrolling pattern prevents the ever-growing DOM issue, allowing a manager to seamlessly scroll from the QB1 down to the absolute lowest-valued undrafted free agent without a single dropped frame.11

## **Visualizing Probabilistic Data and The Uncertainty Envelope**

Sports data, much like financial forecasting, is inherently probabilistic.6 It is derived from incomplete physiological conditions, noisy on-field signals, shifting organizational contexts, and predictive models that produce probability distributions rather than certainties.6 Presenting these highly volatile forecasts as deterministic, single-point numerical estimates mathematically distorts the user's perception of risk and fosters a highly dangerous illusion of precision.6  
Without immediate visual clarity regarding standard error, variance, or confidence intervals, users routinely misinterpret minor statistical fluctuations as meaningful market shifts.6 They will trade away significant assets based on a fractional point projection advantage that exists entirely within the model's margin of error. This leads to overconfident transactions based on deeply flawed baseline assumptions.6 UX is not designed to eliminate this uncertainty; it is designed to explicitly map it, making the invisible math highly visible.6

### **Confidence Bands, PDFs, and Violin Plots**

To accurately convey the variance inherent in dynasty evaluations, the Dynasty Genius UI must construct an explicit "Uncertainty Envelope" around all predictive outputs.2 When displaying longitudinal time-series projections—specifically the projection\_1y through projection\_3y continuous values—the graphical representation must avoid sharp, unadorned linear trajectories.2 Instead, the trajectory lines must be enveloped by graded confidence bands representing 50%, 75%, and 95% probability intervals.2  
For deeper multi-dimensional analysis, particularly when evaluating the Phase 16 college production signals, the interface must deploy probability density functions (PDFs), such as violin plots or ridge plots.2 A violin plot elegantly combines the standard interquartile range mapping of a traditional box plot with a highly detailed kernel density estimation.14 This approach visualizes the exact, nuanced shape of the underlying probability distribution, allowing the user to distinguish instantly between an asset with a tightly clustered, highly stable median outcome and an asset with extreme, bipolar outcome tails.14 The physical width of these density bands must dynamically expand as the forecasting horizon extends into years two and three, visually demonstrating the compounding nature of longitudinal uncertainty.2

### **The Value-Suppressing Uncertainty Palette**

To further reinforce the presence of risk within the standard ranking boards and cross-sectional heat maps, the UI must employ a "Value-Suppressing Uncertainty Palette".2 As the mathematical uncertainty of a specific player projection increases—due to limited professional snap counts, extensive injury histories, severe coaching changes, or experimental model status—the visual saturation of their card and associated metrics must dynamically degrade.2  
This programmed visual "fuzziness" or opacity reduction serves as an immediate, pre-attentive signal to the user that a high median value score carries a massive underlying risk profile.2 A player projecting for 900 Dynasty Value Score points with a 5% margin of error will render in sharp, high-contrast typography. A player projecting for 900 points with a 45% margin of error will render with significantly reduced opacity, inherently signaling instability before the user even reads the underlying numbers.2

### **Contextual Flagging and Experimental States**

Uncertainty without proper contextual grounding often creates decision paralysis rather than operational clarity.6 The UI must clarify the structural root of the variance.6 The system must utilize progressive disclosure, initially presenting simple summary ranges, and allowing the user to hover or expand sections to reveal the underlying model constraints and biological realities driving the variance.5  
If the data constraint relates to incomplete localized positional models, strict programmatic caveat flags must be rendered.2 For example, the profound heterogeneity of the tight end position—where inline blocking specialists and decoupled receiving threats generate entirely distinct, often conflicting analytical profiles—currently prevents unified high-fidelity forecasting.2 Therefore, until the models resolve this variance, all active veteran tight end modules must forcefully append the engine\_b\_experimental\_v1\_fallback tag.2 Furthermore, the UI must hardcode the decision\_supported boolean flag to false for these assets, and deploy warning indicators explicitly mapping the high structural variance, thereby protecting the user from acting on incomplete intelligence.2

## **Roster Context, Team Value Matrix, and League Opportunity Mapping**

The Roster Audit and Evaluation decision surfaces must permanently abandon the display of raw aggregate points in favor of explicit contextual utility.2 Displaying a player's raw projected points mathematically fails to account for the unique supply constraints and rigid lineup demands inherent to fantasy football platforms. Therefore, all roster grids, team value matrices, and league opportunity maps must be anchored strictly to a Value Above Replacement (VAR) index.2

### **Value Above Replacement (VAR) Anchoring**

The UI must globally overlay VAR baselines across every roster in the league to immediately segregate true production outliers from standard replacement-level roster cloggers.2 The system evaluates cross-positional players strictly on their relative advantage over the waiver wire.

| Position | Structural Replacement Baseline (12-Team Superflex PPR) | UI Representation Logic |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Quarterback** | QB25 | Severe VORP multiplier applied due to extreme positional scarcity; sub-QB25 assets flagged as negative utility. |
| **Running Back** | RB33 | Highly volatile baseline; UI tracks localized spike-week potential above replacement. |
| **Wide Receiver** | WR53 | Deep replacement pool; UI heavily penalizes WRs who merely match replacement volume without efficiency. |
| **Tight End** | TE13 | Severe drop-off post-TE12; UI flags massive leverage advantage for elite tier. |

*Table 1: Positional Replacement Baselines for standard 12-Team Superflex PPR formats.* 2  
By anchoring the entire league opportunity map to these baselines, the UI can visually identify macroscopic vulnerabilities across rival rosters. If a competing team has massive capital tied up in wide receivers but is starting the QB28 and QB31, the league opportunity map highlights this structural deficit in high-contrast accent colors, immediately signaling an asymmetric trade target for a manager holding surplus quarterback depth.2

### **The 0-1000 Dynasty Value Score (DVS) and E27 Currency**

To provide appropriate granularity and completely eliminate "tie-clustering" across vast databases of active and developmental players, the Phase 14 architecture mandated the expansion of the Dynasty Value Score (DVS) from a legacy 0-100 scale to a high-resolution 0-1000 continuous integer scale.2  
This 0-1000 mapping functions as the unified, centralized platform currency.2 It inherently synchronizes with the system's E27 (2027 1st-Equivalent) trade calculation logic.2 A premium asset generating a 950 DVS directly and transparently translates into multi-E27 valuations within the transaction models, creating a seamless psychological transition between observing an isolated player value and executing complex, multi-asset trade maneuvers.2 To eliminate residual ties, the interface leverages standard parametric scaling relying on Z-scores and precise Value Over Replacement Player (VORP) multipliers, ensuring every asset occupies a distinct mathematical rank.3

### **Resolving Phase 16 Age Blockers: The Biological Curve UI**

Survivorship bias forces human managers to routinely misprice aging assets, clinging emotionally to historical production while ignoring catastrophic, underlying physiological decay.2 A critical component of the Phase 16 resolution of 2026 age blockers was translating continuous linear biological aging models into severe visual warnings within the UI.2  
The interface must hardcode explicit, highly visible structural decline thresholds.2 These are not gentle suggestions; they are rigid mortality cliffs based on extensive historical datasets.2

* **Running Backs (Age 26):** Traversing Age 26 triggers immediate visual flags detailing compounding soft-tissue injury risk and measurable breakaway speed degradation.2  
* **Wide Receivers (Age 28):** Crossing Age 28 generates UI warnings regarding systematic, irreversible declines in per-route efficiency and target separation.2  
* **Tight Ends (Age 30):** Reaching Age 30 encounters a steep mortality table cliff, heavily suppressing long-term multi-year projections.2  
* **Quarterbacks (Archetype Dependent):** The biological decay matrix is divided. Pocket passers experience a relatively flat production curve peaking at age 30.7, followed by a severe biological cliff specifically hardcoded at Age 33\.3 Conversely, dual-threat quarterbacks operating on the "Konami Code" rushing profile peak much earlier at age 26.1, triggering a rapid "depreciating asset" warning the moment they enter their Age 28 season.3

When evaluating a manager's portfolio via the Team Value Matrix, the UI must aggregate these biological markers.2 Roster configurations overly exposed to post-cliff assets must be visually flagged as highly fragile, utilizing the uncertainty palette to show a rapidly depreciating portfolio floor, thereby prompting immediate liquidation considerations before the external market fully incorporates the depreciation.2

## **The Trade Lab: Mathematical Architecture and Penalty Systems**

The Trade Lab operates as the core transaction modeling interface and the primary decision-support subsystem of Dynasty Genius.1 Moving away from simplistic, additive linear calculators, the Trade Lab utilizes a multi-layered mathematical framework to evaluate transactions based on physical roster constraints, substitution effects, and format-specific utility.1 This fundamentally alters the visual display of trade equity.1  
To prevent systemic calculation timeouts during hyper-dimensional transaction loops, the UI operates under strict hardcoded limits.1

| System Parameter | Hardcoded Value | Operational Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| MAX\_ASSETS\_PER\_SIDE | 6 | Constrains calculations to prevent browser/system timeouts during high-dimensional API matrix loops. |
| FAIRNESS\_THRESHOLD\_PCT | 0.05 (![][image2]) | Establishes the variance band where trades are mathematically flagged as structurally balanced. |
| BASE\_WAIVER\_VALUE\_SF | 1,000 points | Establishes the absolute floor replacement value for a forced roster cut in Superflex formats. |
| Divergence Noise Band (Vet) | 0.10 | Filters out normal market variance to prevent false-positive arbitrage signals on stable veterans. |
| Divergence Noise Band (Rookie) | 0.15 | Widens the variance filter to account for inherent volatility in unproven developmental assets. |

*Table 2: Hardcoded parameters governing the Trade Lab mathematical engine.* 1  
If a trade falls within the tightly controlled 5% variance band, the interface must strictly refuse to indicate a qualitative "Winner" or "Loser".15 Instead, the UI must classify the exchange as purely structural, forcing the user to evaluate the trade based on roster construction needs rather than seeking a minor mathematical victory.15

### **The Double-Sided Roster Cut Penalty**

The traditional exploit of dynasty valuation systems relies on a volume-based arbitrage known as the "ten nickels for a dollar" fallacy.1 Managers consolidate low-tier depth pieces to match the nominal mathematical sum of a single elite superstar. Because actual fantasy rosters operate under strict physical capacity limits, this mathematical equivalence fails entirely in practice.15 A manager receiving an unbalanced package incurs a hidden transaction tax, as they are physically forced to terminate existing bench assets to clear the requisite roster space.15  
To accurately visualize the true cost of asset dilution, the Trade Lab UI must dynamically integrate the Forced-Cut Penalty (![][image3]) microservice.15 Let Team 1 receive a package of players defined as ![][image4], while sending a package defined as ![][image5]. The net expansion or contraction of the roster is calculated by the differential ![][image6].15  
If the calculation indicates that ![][image7], the penalty sequence is initiated.15 The UI must seamlessly query the manager's active Sleeper league roster via API, systematically sort the active bench players (![][image8]) in ascending order based upon their normalized market value (![][image9]), and simulate the mandatory release of the lowest-valued assets.15 The foundational penalty is mathematically formulated as:  
![][image10]  
Here, the system applies the Heaviside step function ![][image11] to ensure the penalty is only active during unbalanced acquisitions 15:  
![][image12]  
The capacity-adjusted net trade value (![][image13]) is subsequently finalized and rendered on the Trade Lab dashboard as 15:  
![][image14]  
This double-sided formulation creates immense strategic clarity on the user interface. It forces a massive, highly visible value deduction against the side accumulating depth, while immediately reducing the penalty to absolute zero for the side consolidating assets.15 The UI must explicitly generate a sub-component card that maps the exact identities of the physical players projected to be cut, reinforcing the tangible roster consequences of the trade structure.15

### **The Competitive Realism Gate and Arbitrage Spotter**

To prevent the visual authorization of deeply skewed theoretical trades that degrade the league's competitive integrity, the Trade Lab incorporates an automated Competitive Realism Gate.1 This subsystem actively intercepts and flags transactions if the incoming package falls below a primary quality threshold (![][image15] of the premier target asset) or triggers an average package dilution threshold (![][image16]).1 When triggered, the UI must physically block the visual confirmation of trade equality, replacing the standard calculation output with a severe dilution warning string.1  
Running continuously beneath the core interface is the Arbitrage Spotter.1 This routine cross-references internal replacement-adjusted values (![][image17]) against external market consensus prices.1 When the deviation exceeds the standard noise bands (0.10 for veterans, 0.15 for rookies), the system flags a high-conviction arbitrage opportunity on the UI.15 By displaying these targets within the context of the error margins, the interface allows the manager to systematically execute value extraction without chasing statistical ghosts.15

## **Engine A, The Rookie Board, and Draft Capital Transforms**

The Rookie Board serves as the dedicated visualization layer for Engine A (the Pre-Professional Prospect Evaluation framework).1 As Dynasty Genius moves from Phase 16 into Phase 17, the prospect presentation strictly mandates a multi-objective bifurcation, resolving the complexities of evaluating college production signals.1

### **Objective A versus Objective B**

The interface must clearly and explicitly demarcate the underlying goal of the visual projection, splitting the UI into two distinct analytical lenses.1  
Objective A represents the Absolute Ranking paradigm.1 This objective mathematically ingests standard NFL draft capital and baseline collegiate production features to replicate, optimize, and synthesize a master consensus draft board.1 It acts as a grounded reference point against general professional market sentiment, providing the safest median projection.1  
In sharp contrast, Objective B isolates the absolute Market Edge through advanced residual variance modeling.1 Objective B explicitly and aggressively excludes both raw NFL draft capital and public Average Draft Position (ADP) from its architecture.1 It focuses purely on non-consensus, highly predictive efficiency signals extracted during the Phase 16 evaluations—such as age-adjusted college production thresholds, specialized combine metrics (e.g., vertical jump for wide receivers), 10-yard splits, and Yards Per Route Run (YPRR) specifically isolated against zone coverages.1  
The UI must cleanly display both the standard rank (Objective A) and the analytical delta (Objective B), using distinct, color-coded indicators to flag exactly where a prospect's true analytical ceiling mathematically diverges from standard market perception.1 By comparing the two, the UI highlights "sleepers" who possess elite underlying traits but lack the consensus pedigree.1

### **The Pick Value Formula and Temporal Weighting**

Assigning static, unchanging values to rookie draft picks generates severe pricing anomalies, traditionally resulting in "pick freezing" where the asset's perceived value stagnates within static calculators for months, failing to reflect real-world liquidity.1 To prevent this structural rigidity, the Rookie Board utilizes a dynamic Temporal Weighting function for draft capital, finalized during the Phase 16 bake-offs.1  
When the actual draft date remains months away, the UI heavily weights the internal, historical baseline projection models, displaying a highly stable, conservative DVS score.1 As the draft approaches and real-time market liquidity exponentially increases, the mathematical formula smoothly transitions the pick's value weighting toward responsive, active transaction data.1 This continuous function ensures that the UI always presents the true real-time purchasing power of draft capital without introducing artificial, overnight shock variables into the team value matrix.1

## **Position-Specific Decision Surfaces: The Quarterback Blueprint**

To seamlessly bridge the analytical gap between Engine A prospects and active Engine B veterans, position-specific player cards must adhere to a strict 65:35 Quantitative-to-Qualitative display discipline.3 The interface cannot devolve into the "mini-resume" anti-pattern—dumping exhaustive, unprioritized lists of traditional box score statistics (e.g., raw passing yards, total interceptions) that lack predictive validity.2 The primary view must be capped at 4 to 5 high-leverage data points, burying raw historical counting stats strictly behind secondary expansion interactions.2

### **Quantitative Anchors and Contextual Signals**

For the Quarterback position, the UI prioritizes highly specific, predictive metrics mapped directly to the 65/35 display rule.3

| Metric Category | Specific UI Data Point | Architectural Significance & Display Rule |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Quantitative (Pro)** | DAKOTA Score | The definitive passing composite. Adjusts EPA per play alongside CPOE (accounting for depth, sideline proximity, separation). Serves as the primary anchor for veteran efficiency. |
| **Quantitative (Rush)** | Konami Code Profile | Separates designed runs from scrambles. Highlights year-over-year stable metrics: Rush Attempts (![][image18]) and Rush Yards (![][image19]). Flags goal-line equity. |
| **Quantitative (College)** | Opponent-Adjusted WEPA | Weighted Expected Points Added via CFBD API. Filters out conference strength noise for Engine A prospects. |
| **Quantitative (Filter)** | Pressure-to-Sack (P2S%) | Critical bust-avoidance metric. P2S% \> 20% triggers a severe UI penalty flag. College sack rates above 8.4% act as severe bust indicators. |
| **Qualitative (Physical)** | BMI Threshold | BMI ![][image20] correlates with top-5 finishes and durability. Hand size is explicitly assigned zero predictive weight and must be stripped from the UI. |
| **Qualitative (Pedigree)** | NFL Draft Capital | Displayed strictly as a discrete categorical string (capital\_tier\_weight e.g., Tier 1: Top 15), never as a raw linear integer. |

*Table 3: The 65:35 Quantitative-to-Qualitative UI display structure for Quarterbacks.* 3  
The UI must prominently feature the Bust-Avoidance Filters.3 If a prospect's P2S% exceeds 20% against top-tier competition, the interface must trigger immediate algorithmic penalty flags, warning the user of the prospect's inability to process pressure.3 The qualitative signals map the biological aging pathways, displaying the explicit cliffs for pocket passers (Age 33\) versus dual-threats (Age 28\) directly on the card to contextually ground the long-term forecast.3

### **The "Dead Window" Routing Fallback**

The architectural routing architecture governing the Player Card UI dictates exactly which engine's data reaches the display surface.3 When querying player.id via the Sleeper UUID, prospects with zero NFL snaps strictly map to Engine A.3 Veterans operating with massive professional data loads strictly route through Engine B.3  
However, players trapped in their first or second season often lack the requisite games\_t minimum threshold for stable Engine B calculations.3 When the system detects a player within this volatile "Dead Window," the UI is forbidden from presenting a highly uncertain Engine B projection as fact. Instead, it must execute the Option B: Explicit Caveat Tagging protocol.3 The visual card outputs the original Engine A prospect baseline score as the foundational prior, but is mandated to forcefully print an exact system string across the header: *"Insufficient professional season data — Engine A prospect score used as prior"*.3 This transparently bridges the gap while maintaining the integrity of the data pipeline.

## **Strict Governance, Identity Matching, and Degraded States**

Integrating Phase 17's full Sleeper universe valuations requires a robust mechanism for measuring structural divergence without corrupting the internal models.1 A fundamental constraint of the system architecture is that external market data must exist as an overlay only.1

### **The Data Pollution Constraint and Market Divergence**

The Phase 9 architecture laid the groundwork for Market Divergence v2 by ingesting standard external metrics—such as FantasyCalc's normalized market\_value consensus price (scaled from 0 to 10,500), overallRank, and the short-term trend\_delta tracking 30-day market velocity.4 However, to maintain absolute structural integrity, these external public datasets must remain entirely restricted to post-processing visual overlays.1 They are completely forbidden from polluting or training the core predictive models.1 If public market sentiment were allowed to influence the internal Engine B projections, the platform would simply mirror the market's existing inefficiencies, destroying its capability to identify structural arbitrage.  
To mathematically bridge the linear outputs of Engine B with the highly skewed, exponential decay characteristics of typical fantasy market pricing, the system relies on the model\_minus\_market\_delta pipeline.4 This metric executes a comprehensive Log-Z transformation, effectively mapping both vectors into a normalized unit of standard deviation.4 By translating these curves, the UI can visually express an exact arbitrage magnitude. Alternatively, the interface tracks Percentile Rank Divergence, directly comparing the internal model percentile against the external market percentile.4 A flag indicating a ![][image21] percentile divergence instantly alerts the manager to a severe structural mispricing, independent of the player's absolute scalar value.4

### **Deterministic Identity Matching: The Anti-Fuzzy Mandate**

Data architecture at this scale requires absolute precision. Identity alignment across the disparate external databases (FantasyCalc, CFBD) and the internal proprietary engines is achieved strictly through the canonical player.sleeperId string.4  
The platform strictly forbids any implementation of fuzzy string matching or heuristic name approximation.1 Attempting to match "G. Davis" to "Gabriel Davis" using Levenshtein distance or probabilistic text matching introduces an unacceptable margin of error, particularly when dealing with rookies, defensive players, or common surnames. The UI must only render data that has achieved a one-to-one deterministic mapping via the UUID to guarantee unassailable data purity.4 If a player lacks a canonical ID mapping, they are deliberately suppressed from the divergence overlays until manually verified.

### **Managing API Failure and Degraded System States**

The UI must anticipate and elegantly handle external infrastructure failures and API latency.4 If the external data scrapers or primary public API endpoints fail to update, the system is explicitly prohibited from rendering stale or decaying market numbers.4  
In such instances, the interface logic mandates setting the market\_value variable immediately to null.4 The decision surface must then display a structured market\_data\_unavailable error banner, effectively shutting down the market delta visual layer until a fresh, validated data stream is re-established.4 It is significantly safer to present no data than to present confidently incorrect, out-of-date data. This rigid enforcement ensures the "Glass Box" philosophy remains untainted by data rot.4  
As technical resources deploy to finalize the frontend architecture for Phase 17, development cycles must remain fiercely concentrated on rendering the backend JSON payloads accurately according to these rules.4 Any deviations into aesthetic front-end polishing outside the parameters of these structured constraints are explicitly banned.4 The user interface exists solely to execute mathematical contracts with absolute fidelity, presenting the highest density of actionable, probabilistic truth possible without succumbing to the human desire for artificial certainty.
